questions.q:
a) Prove that (sin θ + cos θ)^(2) = 1 + sin 2θ.
b) Hence, prove that (sin θ - cos θ)^(2) = 1 - sin 2θ.
questions.answer:
a) We know that (sin θ + cos θ)^(2) = sin^(2) θ + 2 sin θ cos θ + cos^(2) θ.
But from the identity, we know that sin^(2) θ + cos^(2) θ = 1.
Also, 2 sin θ cos θ = sin 2θ.
So, (sin θ + cos θ)^(2) = sin^(2) θ + cos^(2) θ + sin 2θ = 1 + sin 2θ.
b) We can prove (sin θ - cos θ)^(2) by using the identity (sin θ + cos θ)^(2) = 1 + sin 2θ.
If we expand (sin θ - cos θ)^(2) and simplify it, we get (sin θ - cos θ)^(2) = 1 - sin 2θ.
Therefore, (sin θ - cos θ)^(2) = 1 - sin 2θ.
marking_scheme: Expand (sin θ + cos θ)^(2) correctly (1 mark)
Simplify (sin θ + cos θ)^(2) to get 1 + sin 2θ (2 marks)
Prove (sin θ - cos θ)^(2) using the given identity (2 marks)

questions.q:
a) Express sin (A + B) in terms of sin A, sin B, cos A and cos B.
b) Hence, prove that sin (A + B) + sin (A - B) = sin A.
questions.topic: Sine and Cosine of Sum and Difference of Angles
questions.answer:
a) We know that sin (A + B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B.
This is the required expression.
b) We can prove sin (A + B) + sin (A - B) = sin A by using the given expression for sin (A + B).
Let us first expand sin (A + B) + sin (A - B) using the given expression for sin (A + B).
sin (A + B) + sin (A - B) = (sin A cos B + cos A sin B) + (sin A cos B - cos A sin B).
Simplifying this expression, we get sin (A + B) + sin (A - B) = 2 sin A cos B.
Now, let us expand sin 2A using the double angle formula for sine.
sin 2A = 2 sin A cos A.
Therefore, sin (A + B) + sin (A - B) = sin 2A.
But we know that sin 2A = 2 sin A cos A.
Therefore, sin (A + B) + sin (A - B) = sin A.
marking_scheme: Express sin (A + B) in terms of sin A, sin B, cos A and cos B (2 marks)
Prove sin (A + B) + sin (A - B) = sin A using the given expression for sin (A + B) (3 marks)

questions.q:
a) Prove that cos 2A cos 2B - sin 2A sin 2B = cos (2A + 2B).
b) Hence, prove that cos 2A cos 2B - sin 2A sin 2B = cos 2(A + B).
questions.topic: Cosine of Sum and Difference of Angles
questions.answer:
a) We know that cos 2A cos 2B - sin 2A sin 2B = cos (2A + 2B).
This is the required result.
b) We can prove cos 2A cos 2B - sin 2A sin 2B = cos 2(A + B) by using the given result.
Let us first expand cos 2(A + B) using the cosine of sum formula.
cos 2(A + B) = cos 2A cos 2B + sin 2A sin 2B.
But we are given that cos 2A cos 2B - sin 2A sin 2B = cos (2A + 2B).
Therefore, cos 2A cos 2B - sin 2A sin 2B = cos 2(A + B).
marking_scheme: Prove cos 2A cos 2B - sin 2A sin 2B = cos (2A + 2B) (3 marks)
Prove cos 2A cos 2B - sin 2A sin 2B = cos 2(A + B) using the given result (2 marks)

questions.q:
a) Find the value of tan (60° + θ) in terms of tan θ.
b) Hence, prove that tan (60° + θ) = (tan θ + √3)/(1 - tan θ/√3).
questions.topic: Tangent of Sum and Difference of Angles
questions.answer:
a) We know that tan (60° + θ) = (tan 60° + tan θ)/(1 - tan 60° tan θ).
But tan 60° = √3.
Therefore, tan (60° + θ) = (√3 + tan θ)/(1 - √3 tan θ).
b) We can prove tan (60° + θ) = (√3 + tan θ)/(1 - √3 tan θ) by using the given expression for tan (60° + θ).
Let us first simplify the expression (√3 + tan θ)/(1 - √3 tan θ) by multiplying the numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator.
(√3 + tan θ)/(1 - √3 tan θ) = ((√3 + tan θ)(1 + √3 tan θ))/((1 - √3 tan θ)(1 + √3 tan θ)).
Simplifying this expression, we get (√3 + tan θ)/(1 - √3 tan θ) = (√3 + tan θ + √3 tan θ + √3^(2) tan^(2) θ)/(1 - √3^(2) tan^(2) θ).
But we know that 3^(2) = 9.
Therefore, (√3 + tan θ)/(1 - √3 tan θ) = (√3 + tan θ + √3 tan θ + 9 tan^(2) θ)/(1 - 3 tan^(2) θ).
Now, let us expand tan (60° + θ) using the tangent of sum formula.
tan (60° + θ) = (tan 60° + tan θ)/(1 - tan 60° tan θ).
But we know that tan 60° = √3.
Therefore, tan (60° + θ) = (√3 + tan θ)/(1 - √3 tan θ).
Therefore, tan (60° + θ) = (√3 + tan θ + √3 tan θ + 9 tan^(2) θ)/(1 - 3 tan^(2) θ).
Therefore, tan (60° + θ) = (√3 + tan θ)/(1 - √3 tan θ).
marking_scheme: Find the value of tan (60° + θ) in terms of tan θ (2 marks)
Prove tan (60° + θ) = (tan θ + √3)/(1 - tan θ/√3) using the given expression for tan (60° + θ) (3 marks)

questions.q:
a) Find the value of sec (A + B) in terms of sec A, sec B, tan A and tan B.
b) Hence, prove that sec (A + B) = (sec A sec B + tan A tan B)/(1 - tan A tan B sec B).
questions.topic: Secant and Cosecant of Sum and Difference of Angles
questions.answer:
a) We know that sec (A + B) = (cos A cos B + sin A sin B)/(cos A cos B - sin A sin B).
This is the required expression.
b) We can prove sec (A + B) = (sec A sec B + tan A tan B)/(1 - tan A tan B sec B) by using the given expression for sec (A + B).
Let us first expand sec (A + B) using the given expression for sec (A + B).
sec (A + B) = (cos A cos B + sin A sin B)/(cos A cos B - sin A sin B).
Now, let us divide both the numerator and the denominator of the expression by cos A cos B.
sec (A + B) = (1 + tan A tan B)/(1 - tan A tan B).
But we know that tan A = sin A/cos A and tan B = sin B/cos B.
Therefore, sec (A + B) = (1 + (sin A/cos A) (sin B/cos B))/(1 - (sin A/cos A) (sin B/cos B)).
Simplifying this expression, we get sec (A + B) = (1 + (sin A sin B)/(cos A cos B))/(1 - (sin A sin B)/(cos A cos B)).
Therefore, sec (A + B) = ((cos A cos B + sin A sin B)/(cos A cos B))/((cos A cos B - sin A sin B)/(cos A cos B)).
Therefore, sec (A + B) = (sec A sec B + tan A tan B)/(1 - tan A tan B sec B).
marking_scheme: Find the value of sec (A + B) in terms of sec A, sec B, tan A and tan B (2 marks)
Prove sec (A + B) = (sec A sec B + tan A tan B)/(1 - tan A tan B sec B) using the given expression for sec (A + B) (3 marks)
